title: Yellow alert for heavy rain in Himachal amid Boh valley floods - Awaz The Voice
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
full_text:
Shimla
The Shimla Met Office on Wednesday issued a yellow alert for heavy rains in isolated parts of Himachal Pradesh till July 28.
The forecast comes even as three persons were injured, and 12 more houses were damaged in the Boh Valley of Himachal Pradesh's Kangra district amid overnight rain, officials said.
With this, the total number of houses damaged in the valley has climbed to 18. Six houses were washed away in a downpour on Tuesday.
The meteorological department warned of low to moderate flash flood risk in parts of Chamba, Kangra, Mandi, Shimla, Sirmaur and Kullu districts over Wednesday and Thursday.
A cloudburst triggered flash floods and massive debris flows in Sapedi and Garun villages of the valley on Tuesday, with Lam village also staring at similar situation.
Several routes leading to the villages have been affected.
A total of 13 pucca houses and five kutcha houses have been damaged in the rain, and three persons have been injured. Nine cowsheds have also been damaged.
Three buffaloes, four cows, one bull and one sheep died, officials said.
According to reports, residents heard a loud blast-like sound from the hills moments before a powerful torrent of water, mud and boulders rushed downhill.
Timely evacuation of residents is believed to have prevented any loss of life.
Chief Minister Sukhvinder Singh Sukhu spoke over the phone with Kangra Deputy Commissioner Hemraj Bairwa and took feedback on the situation following cloudburst incident.
Sukhu said he would soon visit the cloudburst site to personally assess the damage, adding that his government would provide all possible assistance to those whose houses were damaged in the cloudburst, a statement issued here said.
Presiding over a high-level review meeting to assess the prevailing monsoon situation in the state, Chief Secretary Kamlesh Kumar Pant directed all deputy commissioners (DCs) to remain on high alert.
The chief secretary directed the DCs to maintain the highest level of preparedness and ensure prompt response to any emergency arising due to heavy rainfall, flash floods, landslides, cloudbursts, or other monsoon disasters and closely monitor vulnerable locations and ensure seamless coordination with the state and all line departments.
The DCs briefed the CS on the prevailing conditions in their respective districts and the measures being undertaken to mitigate the impact of monsoon-related disasters.
The Met cautioned that low to moderate flash flood risk is likely over a few watersheds and neighbourhoods of Chamba, Kangra, Mandi, Shimla, Sirmaur and Kullu districts over the next 24 hours.
Moderate to heavy rains lashed parts of Himachal Pradesh, and Mashobra in Shimla district was wettest in the state, recording 128.5 mm of rainfall since Tuesday evening followed by Naina Devi 106.4 mm, Nangal Dam 90 mm, Kufri 77.2, Brahmani 77 mm, and Shimla 69.5 mm.
Several roads in the state have been blocked due to torrential rains over the past few days.

People have been asked to avoid unnecessary travel, stay away from rivers, streams, nallahs and landslide-prone slopes, and refrain from attempting to cross overflowing bridges or flooded roads.

title: Mumbai under yellow alert till July 24 as heavy rain continues, Tansa Lake overflows - Moneycontrol.com
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
full_text:
Mumbai under yellow alert till July 24 as heavy rain continues, Tansa Lake overflows
Mumbai remains under a yellow alert until July 24 as heavy rain continues to lash the city, while neighbouring districts face an orange alert for very heavy rainfall. Rising water levels have pushed the city's reservoir stock to nearly 62%, with Tansa Lake becoming the second lake to overflow this monsoon.
Mumbai remained under a yellow alert on Wednesday as heavy rainfall continued for the third consecutive day, while the India Meteorological Department (IMD) issued an orange alert for Thane, Palghar, Raigad and Ratnagiri, warning of very heavy rainfall over the next 24 hours, as per Indian Express.
The IMD said a low-pressure system over Mumbai and coastal Maharashtra is driving the ongoing spell of moderate to heavy rainfall, which is expected to continue for another day. The department has also forecast gusty winds of 50-60 kmph and a high tide of 3.32 metres around 5.30 pm.
According to the IMD, the Santacruz observatory recorded 48.4 mm of rainfall, while Colaba received 35.6 mm. The Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation's (BMC) automatic weather stations recorded 64.91 mm of rain in the western suburbs, 48.7 mm in the eastern suburbs and 36.65 mm in the island city.
The rainfall has significantly improved Mumbai's water reserves. According to BMC data, the combined water stock in the city's seven lakes has reached 61.9% of total capacity, rising to 8.95 lakh million litres from a capacity of 14.47 lakh million litres. The reserves increased by more than four percentage points in the last 24 hours, as per Indian Express.
Tansa Lake began overflowing on Wednesday morning, becoming the second reservoir to overflow this monsoon after Tulsi Lake earlier this month. Catchment areas received between 65 mm and 224 mm of rainfall during the past 24 hours, with Upper Vaitarna recording the highest rainfall.
Mumbai currently receives around 3,850 million litres of water per day against a demand of 4,200 MLD. Officials said the existing water stock is sufficient to meet the city's requirements for approximately 375 days.
Mumbai draws its drinking water supply from seven reservoirs—Tulsi, Vihar, Bhatsa, Tansa, Upper Vaitarna, Middle Vaitarna and Modak Sagar. While Tulsi and Vihar are located within the city, the remaining lakes are situated across Thane, Palghar and Nashik districts, as per Indian Express.

title: Pradhan unlikely to resign but centre keen on relief measures for kin of victims: Govt functionary - The Economic Times
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
full_text:
New Delhi: Union Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan, who is facing demands for his resignation from protesters and the opposition over the NEET issue, is likely to remain in the Cabinet as the top hierarchy of the government is convinced that he cannot be blamed for the leak of the examination papers, a senior government functionary said.
The government is also ready for serious engagement with the protesters led by the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) and suggested that no one should be rigid about the "venue" of the meeting as insisted by CJP, the functionary said.

Union Health Minister JP Nadda has on Monday held a meeting with two representatives of the CJP, including spokesperson Saurav Das, to listen to their demands and promised to get back after discussing with the senior government leaders.
Since then, there have been a series of meetings within the government involving Union Home Minister Amit Shah, Nadda, Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal, Pradhan, among others.
From the government side, feelers have been sent to the CJP for another meeting on the demands of the outfit.
However, Das told reporters on Wednesday that they are willing to meet Nadda again but at a "neutral venue" near Jantar Mantar and only if the government has an "intention" to accept the demands.
The CJP has demanded that Pradhan should be immediately sacked, and Rs 1 crore compensation should be given to the families of those who committed suicide after the NEET paper leak.
"There is no question of resignation by the education minister. That question does not arise at all. You can't blame the minister for the NEET paper leak as he was not personally involved," the senior functionary said and cited the successful conduct of the re-test.
The government is, however, ready to favourably consider the demand for compensation for the families of those who committed suicide after the NEET paper leak.

The government is also prepared for a detailed discussion on the NEET paper leak issue in Parliament, where it believes it can give an elaborate response to the protests and the action taken by it after the paper leak and "how successfully the re-examination of the medical entrance examination was held".
"If needed, we are ready for discussions even for two days or three days -- whatever time is required. But unrealistic demands of the opposition cannot be met," the functionary said.
Union Minister Jitendra Singh, who met Congress leader Rahul Gandhi when he was staging a protest near the prime minister's residence, accused the leader of opposition of "backtracking" from his earlier demand for a debate on NEET paper leak in Parliament.
Singh alleged that Gandhi later demanded Pradhan's resignation as a pre-condition.
On Wednesday, Gandhi again called for Pradhan's resignation, besides other demands which he said were "non-negotiable".
Sources said, as part of the government's outreach to the agitating students and youth, Nadda visited Ram Manohar Lohia hospital and met the protesters who were injured on July 20.
The health minister, along with minister Jitendra Singh, also visited activist Sonam Wangchuk at the Medanta Hospital in Gurugram on Tuesday night.
Wangchuk has been on an indefinite hunger strike since June 28 after joining the CJP-led agitation demanding accountability over alleged irregularities in competitive examinations, reforms in the education system and the resignation of Pradhan.
He was earlier forcibly shifted to the Safdarjung Hospital, and is currently admitted to the Medanta hospital after being shifted following a Delhi High Court order.
The CJP has been spearheading protests over alleged examination irregularities since June.
Protesters participating in the CJP's Parliament march on July 20 refused to disperse despite heavy police barricading, lathicharge and use of tear gas.
The march, which witnessed participation of students and people from across Delhi, was stopped near Parliament Street, where police had put up multiple layers of barricades.
